title: Cat Hyperactive with Open-Mouth Panting: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat panting after running? Open-mouth breathing in cats is a high-priority emergency. Learn why and what to do immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Open-mouth panting after hyperactivity in cats is a critical medical emergency signaling severe respiratory or cardiac distress. Stop all activity and transport your cat to an emergency veterinarian immediately.

What does it mean if my cat is hyperactive and then starts panting with its mouth open?

When a cat exhibits intense hyperactivity followed by open-mouth breathing or panting, it is a sign that their body is struggling to meet oxygen demands or regulate temperature. Unlike dogs, cats are not 'efficient' panters. In a feline, this behavior often indicates that the heart or lungs are under significant stress, or the cat has reached a dangerous level of overexertion or overheating.

Is my cat's open-mouth panting a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Open-mouth panting in a cat is always considered a medical emergency until proven otherwise.
  • This combination of symptoms can be a precursor to congestive heart failure, feline asthma, or heatstroke.
  • If your cat is currently panting, you should minimize stress, stop all play, and transport them to the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately.

How can taking a video of my cat's breathing help the emergency vet?

  • If you can safely record a short 10-second video of your cat's breathing and movement without delaying your departure, it can be life-saving.
  • A video allows the triage team to see the specific effort of the breath, the movement of the chest wall, and the cat's posture.
  • This information helps the veterinary team determine if your cat needs immediate oxygen therapy the moment you walk through the door.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Cats exhibiting hyperactivity and open-mouth panting require immediate veterinary attention. Hyperactivity may stem from paradoxical reactions to drugs like diazepam, potentially leading to fatal hepatic failure with repeated use. Hyperactivity can also result from serotonin syndrome, indicated by altered mental status, agitation, myoclonus, hyperreflexia, tremors, diarrhea, incoordination, cardiovascular changes, and fever, often triggered by antidepressants or stimulants. Acepromazine or chlorpromazine may be used to control hyperactivity. The animal should be closely monitored for responsiveness and breathing, with IV fluids administered to support blood pressure.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3034)

faqs.question: Do cats ever pant when they are just happy or tired?
faqs.answer: Generally, no. While a very brief moment of panting after extreme play in a young kitten might occur, it is still considered abnormal. Any cat breathing with an open mouth should be evaluated by a professional.

faqs.question: What should I look for in my cat's mouth?
faqs.answer: Check the color of the tongue and gums. If they appear blue, purple, or very pale instead of a healthy bubblegum pink, your cat is not getting enough oxygen.

faqs.question: What can I do on the way to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the car cool with air conditioning and keep the carrier level. Avoid talking loudly or playing music, as reducing stress is vital for a cat in respiratory distress.
